danger_signals.visual_form: Eastern Bloc countries isolated on map/interface with restricted trade routes
danger_signals.meaning: Geopolitical hostility blocks aircraft purchases and reduces route availability

danger_signals.visual_form: Random civil war notifications tied to specific cities (e.g., Lima, Peru)
danger_signals.meaning: Regional instability reduces passenger traffic and profitability in affected areas

danger_signals.visual_form: Historical event trigger (1973, mid-late 2010s); likely affects fuel costs/airline viability
danger_signals.meaning: Economic shock that threatens airline operations during vulnerable periods

danger_signals.visual_form: Random event notifications (volcanic eruption New Zealand, floods Thailand/Vancouver, earthquake São Paulo)
danger_signals.meaning: Infrastructure damage disrupts routes and passenger flows in affected regions

danger_signals.visual_form: Supersonic aircraft become rapidly obsolete (2007–2016 in game timeline)
danger_signals.meaning: Fleet investments become worthless if timing is poor; signals technological shift danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Era selection 1985–2005 described as 'economic prosperity and relative stable peace'
safety_signals.meaning: Stable conditions favor airline growth and investment returns

safety_signals.visual_form: Post-1986 perestroika unlocks Eastern Bloc access to Western aircraft suppliers
safety_signals.meaning: Diplomatic thaw expands market opportunities and reduces geopolitical constraints

safety_signals.visual_form: Growth of European Union visualized through country integration (hypothetical: Russia joins EU 2005)
safety_signals.meaning: Regional consolidation reduces trade barriers and increases route viability

safety_signals.visual_form: Olympic Games scheduled in major cities (accurate through Sydney 2000, then randomized)
safety_signals.meaning: Predictable surge in passenger demand during Olympic periods provides revenue opportunity

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Aircraft represented as undersized, inefficient gas-guzzlers with limited medium-range capabilities
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Isolated trade relationships until 1986; restricted from Boeing/Lockheed/McDonnell-Douglas; access to Airbus/Sud Aviation at markup; normal-excellent relations with Africa, Middle East, Central/South America
Tense relations with Eastern Europe until ~1985; access to premium aircraft makers; competitive advantage in North Atlantic routes
Home to Boeing, Lockheed, McDonnell-Douglas; hostile relations with Eastern Bloc; competitive hub regions include North America
Hong Kong retrocession event (1997); gradual opening to international trade

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Eastern Bloc countries displayed with restricted trade connections; Western suppliers inaccessible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Eastern airlines forced to use inefficient domestic aircraft; cannot form transatlantic/transpacific routes; limited competitive reach

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Eastern Bloc access restrictions lift; aircraft price drops for US suppliers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Eastern airlines gain access to Western aircraft; can compete in previously closed markets

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Further price reductions for Eastern aircraft; Berlin Wall destruction event
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Former Eastern Bloc airlines integrate into global competition; new route opportunities open

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Reduced geopolitical friction; normal trading relations across blocs
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Airlines compete on efficiency and service rather than political access; global route networks feasible

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ukraine, Belarus, Switzerland, Russia join EU (game timeline); Airbus prices drop for Eastern suppliers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Continental European integration reduces trade barriers; Eastern airlines gain cost advantages

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Historical event notification; likely UI indicator of fuel cost escalation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Airlines must adjust routes and fleet composition to maintain profitability; unprofitable routes become visible losses

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Supersonic aircraft available for purchase; aircraft type UI shows rapid obsolescence cycle
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Supersonic aircraft provide temporary competitive edge (2007–2016) then become worthless; forces fleet renewal decisions

environmental_storytelling.example: Suez Crisis, Indo-Pakistani War, Yom Kippur War, Iran–Iraq War, Desert Storm occur during play; affect regional stability and route viability without explicit narrative text
Downfall of colonialism, Singapore ejection from Malaysia, Hong Kong retrocession: cities change geopolitical status, affecting airline relationships and market access
1955–1975 era shows dawn of jet airplanes; 2000–2020 depicts supersonic replacement; visual aircraft roster reflects historical/speculative tech progression
Eastern Bloc isolation vs. post-1986 integration; EU expansion; Germany reunification: game map/relationship matrix visually communicates political alignment changes
Volcanic eruption (New Zealand), floods (Thailand, Vancouver), earthquake (São Paulo), civil wars (Lima): disasters anchor climate/political instability to specific locations on game map

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Limited to undersized, inefficient aircraft; cannot purchase from Western suppliers; confined to medium-range regional routes; competitive only in African/Middle Eastern/Central American markets
Gain access to US aircraft at standard pricing; can expand range and efficiency; begin competing in Western markets
Ilyushin/Tupolev aircraft become more efficient; Airbus pricing drops; achieve cost parity with Western competitors
Exclusive access to premium aircraft (Boeing, Lockheed, McDonnell-Douglas); can form global route networks; competitive dominance in North Atlantic/transpacific routes
Fuel cost escalation visible in profitability; unprofitable routes become apparent; must adjust fleet or pricing
Supersonic fleet offers temporary competitive advantage; rapid obsolescence forces reinvestment decisions; obsolete aircraft become sunk costs
Routes in affected cities experience passenger/profit loss; regional hub value decreases temporarily; routes may become unviable

notes: Aerobiz Supersonic communicates world-state primarily through mechanical constraints (aircraft access, trade restrictions, pricing) rather than visual aesthetics. The manifestation layer operates as geopolitical simulation: Cold War friction is encoded as supplier access denial; normalization is encoded as price reductions and route unlocking. Environmental storytelling relies on historical event notifications anchored to real-world dates and locations. Faction identity is expressed through aircraft capability (Eastern = inefficient, Western = premium) and trade relationship matrices rather than color or symbol. The game's paradigm emphasizes systemic manifestation: players read world-state through what is mechanically possible at each historical moment, not through explicit visual design. Danger signals manifest as economic shocks (oil crises, aircraft obsolescence) and geopolitical isolation (pre-1986 Eastern Bloc). Safety signals manifest as diplomatic normalization, economic stability periods, and predictable revenue events (Olympics). The 1985–2005 'prosperity era' is the safest world-state; earlier eras (Cold War) and later eras (climate crisis, supersonic obsolescence) introduce systemic danger.
